text: Hello, my name is Jose Pimiento, and I'm one of the surgical oncologists at Moffitt Cancer Center. I have a video for you guys today. It's the robotic total gastrectomy for cancer in the background of CDH-I mutation. The case is one of a thirty-year-old with diagnosis of a pathogenic CDH-I mutation in a screening endoscopy. It was found to have foci of invasive signet-ring cell cancer in random biopsies. No clear family history for invasive gastric cancer and imaging studies did not reveal neural disease or metastasis.

The surgeon places the ports, including a twelve millimeter robotic port for stapling and extraction, and begins by dividing the gastrocolic ligament and left gastroepiploic vessel. Using a vessel sealer, the short gastric vessels are dissected and divided. Care is taken to avoid injury to the redundant splenic artery. The attachments of the greater curvature to the diaphragm are dissected to mobilize the proximal stomach.

The surgeon mobilizes the distal stomach and dissects the right gastrointestinal vessels, removing the lymph node package and dividing the vessels with a vascular stapler. The lesser curve is dissected, and the right gastric vessels are divided using a vessel sealer. The duodenum is carefully divided with a blue load stapler, avoiding injuries. The pars flaccida is dissected, and the right crux is identified. Lymph nodes associated with the celiac trunk and left gastric artery are removed, exposing the anterior surface of the right and left crura. The splenic artery is taken off, including associated lymphatics.

The surgeon dissects the left gastric vessels, leaving hepatic artery lymph nodes for later removal, and divides the left gastric vessels with a vascular stapler, ensuring the celiac trunk is not narrowed. The hiatus is dissected to free the esophagus, avoiding injury to the intrathoracic esophagus intended for anastomosis. Once the esophagus is exposed and dissected, it is divided with a blue load stapler. The specimen, including the omentum, is placed in a large endocash bag, with proximal and distal margins sent for frozen section analysis, ensuring esophagus at the proximal margin and duodenum at the distal margin.

The surgeon completes the lymphadenectomy of the hepatic artery periportal territory using a vessel sealer, placing the specimen in a separate retrieval bag. The hepatic artery, celiac trunk, takeoff of the splenic artery, and a portion of the portal vein are fully exposed. The ligament of Treitz is identified, and the small bowel is divided with a blue-load stapler. The mesentery is divided to create the Roux limb, ensuring adequate vascularization and freeing the bowel to reach the hiatus.

The surgeon uses Firefly technology to confirm the vascularity of the bowel. They measure 55 centimeters and create two small enterotomies to perform an enteroenterostomy using a blue load stapler. The anastomosis is confirmed, and the common channel is closed with a running 3-0 V-Loc suture in a Cornell type pattern. The mesenteric defect created during the formation of the Roux limb is closed. The Orvil device is then placed in the divided esophagus using robotic scissors, taking care not to injure the esophagus.

The surgeon uses a size 25 anvil to remove the staple line from the prelim and places an extra-long size XXV EEA to create an anastomosis between the esophagus and bowel, ensuring the bowel is not twisted. The EEA is removed, and the completeness of the donuts is checked. The bowel is divided with a blue load stapler, three centimeters from the anastomosis. An endoscopy and leak test are performed under direct visualization, followed by the placement of an isogastric tube and a drain to cover the posterior mediastinum and divided duodenum.
The pathology as stated with thirty negative lymph nodes. Thank you very much for your time and this is our team at Moffitt. If you have any additional questions, please feel free to contact us.
